item.title: Database isolation levels and locking
item.author: Vaishali Behere
item.content:encoded: Understanding database locking and isolation levelsIn this article, the main focus is exploring the depths of the ‘whys’ and ‘hows’ of the ‘I’ (Isolation) in ACID properties of relational databases. The Isolation property ensures that two concurrent transactions do not cause data inconsistencies by interfering with each other. There are different isolation levels provided by the database as part of this property. The higher the isolation level, the better the protection against inconsistencies, but at the cost of overall performance.Let’s first understand the issues with concurrent transactions in a world where there are no locks and no isolation levels to protect them.Frequently discussed issues:There are two additional critical issues that are rarely discussed:Dirty writes (Never happens! As even the lowest isolation level provides protection from it. We will discuss in detail later in the doc.): The end result of these operations should have been what Transaction 2 intended to do, i.e., read the current price of item A and increment it by 50 because Transaction 1 never made it to the DB. The expected value was 150. Since Transaction 2 read the uncommitted value of Transaction 1 (which later rolled back), the data inconsistency caused the price to increase by 100 instead of the intended increase of 50. Replace ‘price’ with financial accounts, and a dirty read becomes a sin!: Transaction 1 read the quantity of item A. In the meantime, another transaction intervened, set the quantity to a different value, and committed. Transaction 1 would not be able to read the same value it read at Step 1 again. Is this really a big deal? Why is this being called an issue? Isn’t this the right behavior? Obviously, you should read the committed value the second time you access it. However, this leads to financial disputes and customer dissatisfaction when a transaction based on the original value is altered in the middle. Therefore, it needs to be addressed effectively wherever applicable.Phantom reads (not covering in depth): The phantom reads problem occurs primarily due to new row insertions and not due to updates on the same row. For instance, if a concurrent transaction inserts new rows and a re-read range query is issued, it returns a different number of rows: Consider a scenario where an item’s stock quantity was 100. Customer 1 and Customer 2 came in together, and Customer 1 cancelled an order for 10 items, resulting in the quantity being returned to stock. At the same time, another customer placed an order for 100 items, resulting in the quantity being deducted from stock. Ultimately, item A should have 10 items left in the warehouse. But due to Transaction 1’s updates getting lost, 10 items can never be retrieved, despite the item being available.Dirty writes(not covering in depth because even the lowest of isolation level prevents it)Issue: Expected value should have been what transaction1 intended to do i.e., A=150 as transaction2 never got committed, but dirty writes would cause A’s value to be 200.Different type of isolation levels in relational databasesWhich issues can/cannot happen in which isolation levels - NoCannot happen- Yes — Can happenLock and lock rules (MySQL with default InnoDB engine is taken as a reference) :Let’s first understand some terminologies related to lockingIntention locks are table level non blocking locks indicating intention of a transaction to inform concurrent transactions that there is a future intent to acquire granular row level locks so that data consistency can be maintained. (IS) = Intent shared locks, (IX) = Intent exclusive locks.Shared Locks (Read locks)Shared locks are the locks acquired for reading the row. It is represented by (S)Exclusive Locks (Write locks) Exclusive locks are the locks acquired for modifying the row. One such example is update command. It is represented by (X)Locking reads are the reads taking IS or IX on select command. Mentioned in detail in Intention lock section.Consistent non locking reads : Consistent non locking reads happen when a read operation is performed using snapshot info. Snapshot is a particular point in time state of the actual table.Let’s now understand the constant rules across any isolation levels that bind different locks together :Let’s now understand the variable rules across isolation levels that help them solve different concurrent transaction problems : of a row doesn’t involve reading from snapshot i.e. consistent read. areheld until end of transaction.even within the same transaction resets and reads fresh snapshot i.e. if any concurrent transaction commits the updates, consecutive consistent read in the original transaction will have updated data. One important thing to keep in mind is that the default mode for plain SELECT queries in this isolation level is non-locking reads, i.e., no lock is acquired for reads.areheld until end of transaction.Every consistent read in a transaction reads from a snapshot of the committed changes taken when the first consistent read command was issued. i.e., if any concurrent transaction commits updates, consecutive consistent reads in the original transaction will always read the data it initially read. The only exception is reading the updated value in the next read if updates are performed within the same transaction. One important thing to keep in mind is that the default mode for plain SELECT queries in this isolation level is non-locking reads, i.e., no lock is acquired for reads.areheld until end of transaction.If autocommit is off, it converts non-locking reads, i.e., plain SELECTs, to locking reads automatically, i.e., SELECT ... FOR SHARE. If autocommit is on, every plain SELECT is a transaction on its own and hence treated as a non-locking read.areheld until end of transaction.Let’s use constant rules and variable rules together for each isolation level to understand how different concurrent transaction issues are tackledRead uncommitted / Read committed / Repeatable read / Serializable : Dirty writes is not possible in any isolation level because at step2, once X lock is acquired, it will be held until end of T1. Hence, T2 can never set A to the new value and will have to wait as T1 holds X so no other transaction can hold X till T1 commits/rollbacks.T2 continues it’s decisions basis A=150 : Since there is no reading from snapshot, T2 (at Step3) can read the intermediate value set by T1 (at Step2) hence dirty reads.Read committed / Repeatable read : Since T1 takes X lock at Step2, and since the refreshed/first read snapshot of T2 has only committed data, T2 at Step3 would still read A=100 hence no dirty reads. The problem of lost updates might arise (will be discussed later in the section) if step4 is commit instead of rollback, but definitely not dirty reads. : Since T1 will take X lock at step2 and will hold it until EOT, T2 will be blocked at Step3 due to locking read not compatible with write lock and will have to wait for read until T1 releases the lock at the end of transaction. Hence, dirty reads are prevented.Non repeatable reads recap : Since there is no reading from snapshot, T1 at step1 and step4 will have different values read. Hence, non repeatable read issue arises. : Since snapshot is refreshed for every read, T1 at step1 and step4 will have different values read. Hence, non repeatable read issue arises. : Since during first read of T1 at Step1, snapshot is finalised, another read at Step4 will have the same value read at Step1. Hence, it solves non repeatable read issue. : Since T1 at step1 will be converted to locking read despite it being the plain select, T2 at step2 will anyway have to wait for T1 to finish hence no chance of non repeatable read issue.Skipping this as it deals with inserting new rows altogether and not working on concurrent updates to the same row. This can only be prevented with Serializable isolation level due to gap locks (not in scope as part of this article).T1 reads A=100 , T2 reads A=100 at the same timeT1 adds quantity to A by 10 i.e. A=110T2 deducts quantity from A by 100 i.e. A=0Read uncommitted / Read committed / Repeatable read : Since step1 has non locking reads, step2,3,4,5 can execute without any hinderance causing lost updates issue. : Since T1, T2 at step1 will be locking reads, T1 needing X at step2 will be blocked on T2 to release IS lock acquired at step1 and T2 needing X at step4 will be blocked on T1 to release IS lock acquired at step1 and IS lock release is not going to happen in any transaction until EOT . It is a deadlock! InnoDB behavior would be that T2 will end by getting a at Step4 and T1 will go through. Hence, lost updates issue is prevented.Frequently used SQL commands to keep handy for testing different use casesset session transaction isolation level read uncommitted; --To set session isolation levelselect @@transaction_isolation --To check the set session isolation levelselect @@global.transaction_isolation --To check global isolation levelshow engine innodb status; --To check if any transaction is waiting on a lockstart transaction; --To start a transactionselect * from x where y=z for share; --To get IS lockselect * from x where y=z for update; --To get IX lockrollback; --To rollback the transactioncommit; --To commit the transactionNote — All the examples mentioned in the article have been practically tried and tested.More on deadlocks and solving the lost updates problem without using the serializable isolation level in the next article.

item.title: How to Run MySQL in a Docker Container
item.author: Dbschema Pro
item.content:encoded: In this article, we will see a step-by-step tutorial on running a MySQL database in a Docker Container.1. Downloading a MySQL Server Docker ImageTo download the image, open the command line and type this command:docker pull mysql/mysql-server:latestThe: latest tag will download the latest version of MySQL. If you want do download a specific version, simply replace the (Ex: mysql-server: 8.0)2. Start a MySQL Container in DockerThe next step is to run a container in Docker with the MySQL image. To do this, execute the next command:docker run --name=mysql1 -p 3306:3306 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=123456 -d mysql/mysql-server:8.0Let’s break down this command to understand it better:run — will run a new command in a new Docker container–name — will give a name to the new container created-p — will make the internal docker port visible outside docker***-e** — will change the root password. Here you can insert whatever password you wantmysql/mysql-server:8.0 — will specify what image to run in the newly created containerTo verify if the container was created and running, we can execute a docker ps (process status) command:CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES79742fe73ea9 mysql/mysql-server:8.0 "/entrypoint.sh mysq…" 24 hours ago Up 6 seconds (health: starting) 3306/tcp, 33060/tcp mysql1This will list all the running containers as shown above.You can see the (health: starting) mention in the status column of the above result. After the container is initializedand ready to run, you will see it change to (healthy).3. Connecting to MySQL Server from within the containerTo connect, we will first run the next command:docker exec -it mysql1 mysql -uroot -pThis will require the root password set in the previous step. After inserting the password, you should be inside MySQLmonitor.Type to leave the program.4. Stopping the MySQL containerTo stop the container, simply execute the next command:5. Deleting the MySQL containerTo delete the container, make sure that it is stopped. Then, execute this command:This article was written by the DbSchema team — a database design and management tool that supports MySQL. With DbSchema, you can design database diagrams, generate interactive documentation, and manage your database easily. Download and try it here.

item.title: Create New User in MySQL & Grant Access
item.author: Dbschema Pro
item.content:encoded: Creating a new user in MySQL and granting privileges is very easy. First, you have to open the MySQL command line.You can do this by reaching the bin directory from Program Files.cd\Program Files\MySQL\MySQL Server 8.0\binThis is the usual path for the MySQL bin folder. After you reach the bin folder, execute the next command:This will require the password set at the installation.In MySQL, users are a combination of __username`` and __hostname identified by a __`password. The __hostname`` can be replaced with __%`` to allow connections from any host.3. Create a user for ‘localhost’Now, from inside the MySQL shell, let’s create a new user for __`localhost`` by executing the next command:CREATE USER 'user'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';To grant access to the user, execute:GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'user'@'localhost';Finally, we will reload the grant table by executing:Now let’s break down the commands to understand them better:__CREATE USER ‘user’@’localhost’ - pretty straight forward, this command will tell MySQL to create a new user. Replace __user withthe name you want to attribute. __@’localhost’ specifies that the user can connect only from __localhost``;__IDENTIFIED BY ‘password’` — here we specify the password attributed to that user;__GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES - this will give the user rights to modify the tables, columns, etc. The __ALL PRIVILEGES part specifiesthat the user will have standard privileges;__ON - this part specifies the databases on which the user will have access. The first * means that the user will have access to **all databases**. If you want to grant access on a specific database, replace it with the name. For example,ON dbtest.*`;__TO ‘user’@’localhost’` — identifies the user;__FLUSH PRIVILEGES` — this will tell the server to refresh the grant tables.4. Create a user that can connect from any hostTo give a user the right to connect from any host, we have to replace the __localhostmentions from the previous commands with%`. They will look like this:CREATE USER 'user'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'user'@'%';5. Delete a user from the databaseTo delete a user from the database, we will execute the next command:DROP USER 'user'@'localhost'Now you are ready to manage your users in MySQL.If you’d like to improve your MySQL database design and management, please try DbSchema for free.

item.title: Automating Multi-Node Docker Swarm Deployment With Terraform & Ansible
item.author: Noel
item.content:encoded: Managing containerized applications at scale requires a robust and automated provisioning mechanism. While Docker Swarm simplifies container orchestration, setting up and configuring Swarm clusters across multiple nodes manually can be -: Inconsistent configurations lead to deployment failures.: Manually provisioning instances, setting up networking, and initializing Swarm takes significant effort.: Scaling and upgrading the cluster without automation introduces operational risks.To address these challenges, Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform and Ansible provide a powerful combination - handles infrastructure provisioning (e.g., creating VMs, configuring networking). automates post-provisioning configuration (e.g., installing Docker, initializing Swarm, setting up HashiCorp Vault, monitoring, and logging). provides container orchestration, enabling self-healing, service discovery, and load balancing across multiple worker nodes.This article provides a step-by-step guide to deploying a highly available, multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform and Ansible. The key components include -✅ Terraform for Infrastructure ProvisioningCreating cloud instances (e.g., OpenStack, AWS, Azure, GCP).Defining infrastructure components like security groups, networking, and storage.✅ Ansible for Configuration ManagementInstalling Docker & setting up Swarm managers and worker nodes.Configuring service discovery, networking, and monitoring.✅ Docker Swarm for Container OrchestrationRunning applications in a multi-node cluster.Deploying and managing services across worker nodes.✅ High Availability (HA) ConsiderationsImplementing multi-manager node setups for fault tolerance.Using HashiCorp Vault for secrets management.Integrating log aggregation and monitoring for real-time insights.By the end of this article, you’ll learn to implement a fully automated multi-node Docker Swarm cluster running on OpenStack (or any cloud provider), ready for production workloads by following the exact same methods and best practices our DevOps teams at Xinthe do.Understanding The Tools — Terraform, Ansible & Docker SwarmDeploying a highly available, automated Docker Swarm cluster requires the integration of three key technologies — Terraform, Ansible, and Docker Swarm. Each of these tools serves a distinct purpose in ensuring scalability, reliability, and automation of the infrastructure and orchestration layer.1️⃣ Terraform — Infrastructure-As-Code (IaC)Terraform is a declarative infrastructure provisioning tool that enables developers and DevOps teams to define cloud resources in a repeatable, scalable, and version-controlled manner.: Works across major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP, OpenStack).: Tracks changes to infrastructure, preventing manual drifts.: Reusable modules streamline provisioning at scale.✅ Terraform In Docker Swarm DeploymentProvision Compute Instances: Launches Swarm manager and worker nodes.: Defines security groups, firewall rules, and virtual networks.: Allocates volumes for persistent data storage.: Reduces manual intervention in infrastructure setup.Example: Terraform Configuration for launching Swarm nodes on OpenStack -resource "openstack_compute_instance_v2" "swarm_node" { name = "swarm-manager" image_name = "Ubuntu-22.04" flavor_name = "m1.medium" key_pair = "swarm-key" security_groups = ["default"] name = "swarm-network"}In this case, Terraform provisions the infrastructure while Ansible will configure it.2️⃣ Ansible — Configuration Management & AutomationAnsible is a declarative, agentless automation tool designed to ensure consistent and idempotent configuration across multiple nodes.: Uses SSH to push configurations.: Ensures the same configuration is applied every time without unintended changes.: Supports roles and reusable playbooks.✅ Ansible In Docker Swarm DeploymentInstalls Docker Engine on all nodes.Initializes the Swarm Cluster and assigns nodes as managers/workers.Deploys Monitoring & Logging Tools (e.g., Prometheus, ELK Stack).Example: Ansible Playbook for installing Docker -- name: Install Docker hosts: all tasks: - name: Install dependencies apt: name: ['apt-transport-https', 'ca-certificates', 'curl', 'software-properties-common'] state: present - name: Add Docker repository shell: curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | apt-key add - apt: state: presentOnce Terraform provisions the nodes, Ansible automates software installation and cluster setup.3️⃣ Docker Swarm — Native Container OrchestrationDocker Swarm is a lightweight alternative to Kubernetes for managing containerized applications. It provides service discovery, fault tolerance, and auto-scaling with less complexity compared to Kubernetes.Built-in with Docker (No extra installation required).Lightweight & Simple: Ideal for small-to-medium-scale workloads.Supports Rolling Updates & Load Balancing automatically.Distributed & Fault-Tolerant: Multiple managers ensure high availability.✅ Docker Swarm In Our Deployment: Control the cluster and schedule services.: Execute containers and respond to scaling needs.: Distributes traffic across replicas.: Ensures seamless application upgrades.Example: Swarm Initialization using Ansible -- name: Initialize Docker Swarm hosts: manager - name: Create Swarm command: docker swarm init --advertise-addr {{ ansible_default_ipv4.address }}After initialization, worker nodes will join the cluster dynamically, ensuring scalability and high availability.Combining these tools helps us achieve a fully automated, scalable, and production-ready Swarm cluster with minimal manual intervention.Designing A Scalable Multi-Node Docker Swarm ArchitectureA highly available (HA) Docker Swarm cluster requires careful planning of infrastructure components, security, and networking. This section outlines the key components, best practices for security, and networking considerations to ensure a resilient and scalable deployment.1️⃣ Infrastructure ComponentsA multi-node Swarm cluster consists of manager and worker nodes, along with optional load balancers and monitoring nodes for increased performance, reliability, and observability.Cluster Topology OverviewKey Considerations For Each ComponentControl Swarm membership, service scheduling, and high availability.Use odd-numbered clusters (3, 5, or 7 managers) to prevent split-brain scenarios.Deploy managers in different availability zones (if on the cloud) for fault tolerance.Execute containers and scale dynamically based on workload.Use autoscaling (AWS ASG, Azure VM Scale Sets, GCP Managed Instance Groups) to handle traffic bursts.Workers can be safely rebooted without impacting cluster integrity.c) Load Balancer (Optional, But Recommended)NGINX, HAProxy, or AWS ALB can act as the external traffic entry point.Routes requests to Swarm services via Docker’s built-in ingress network.Ensures high availability and failover handling.d) Monitoring Node (Optional, But Recommended)Prometheus collects metrics on CPU, memory, and service health.Grafana visualizes the Swarm cluster’s performance in real-time.Log aggregation with ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) enables debugging.2️⃣ Security & Networking ConsiderationsA secure Docker Swarm deployment ensures data integrity, encrypted communications, and controlled access to services.🔐 Security Best Practices🌐 Networking Best PracticesBy default, Docker Swarm operates with an internal overlay network to connect services across nodes securely. However, optimizing firewall rules and port access is essential for performance and security.Restrict 2377 to only manager nodes to prevent unauthorized access.Ensure worker nodes only expose 7946 and 4789 for internal communication.Use Cloud Provider Firewalls / AWS Security Groups to block all public access except via the load balancer.Terraform Example — Secure Network ConfigurationThe following Terraform configuration provisions a secure VPC and security group for Swarm nodes -resource "aws_vpc" "swarm_vpc" { cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"resource "aws_subnet" "swarm_private_subnet" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24" map_public_ip_on_launch = falseresource "aws_security_group" "swarm_sg" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id from_port = 2377 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] # Allow only internal communication } from_port = 7946 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] } from_port = 4789 protocol = "udp" cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] } from_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] # Allow outgoing connections }Provisioning Infrastructure With TerraformIn this section, we will provision a highly available multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform. The infrastructure will be deployed in AWS, but the approach can be adapted for GCP, Azure, or on-premise environments.1️⃣ Setting Up The Terraform Project StructureA well-structured Terraform project ensures maintainability and modularity. Below is a recommended directory structure for deploying Docker Swarm with Terraform and Ansible.terraform-docker-swarm/│── main.tf # Defines AWS/GCP infrastructure│── variables.tf # Input variables for customization│── outputs.tf # Outputs like IP addresses│── ansible/ # Ansible playbooks for configuration│── inventory.ini # Hosts inventory for Ansible│── deploy.sh # Deployment script → Provisions AWS infrastructure (instances, networking, security groups). → Stores configurable inputs (e.g., region, instance type, AMI ID). → Captures instance IPs for further automation with Ansible. → Contains playbooks for setting up Docker Swarm. → Automates Terraform deployment and Ansible execution.2️⃣ Terraform Code For Provisioning Multi-Node InfrastructureTerraform will be used to provision EC2 instances, configure security groups, and output the necessary information for Ansible.🔹 main.tf (Example for AWS EC2 Instances)provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1"resource "aws_vpc" "swarm_vpc" { cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"resource "aws_subnet" "swarm_subnet" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24" map_public_ip_on_launch = trueresource "aws_security_group" "swarm_sg" { vpc_id = aws_vpc.swarm_vpc.id # Allow SSH for Ansible automation ingress { to_port = 22 cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] # Allow Docker Swarm traffic ingress { to_port = 2377 cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] from_port = 7946 protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] } from_port = 4789 protocol = "udp" cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"] } from_port = 0 protocol = "-1" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] }resource "aws_instance" "swarm_nodes" { count = 5 ami = "ami-12345678" # Ubuntu 22.04 LTS AMI ID instance_type = "t3.medium" key_name = "swarm-key" subnet_id = aws_subnet.swarm_subnet.id vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.swarm_sg.id] Name = "Swarm-Node-${count.index + 1}" provisioner "remote-exec" { inline = ["sudo apt-get update -y"] type = "ssh" private_key = file("~/.ssh/swarm-key.pem") host = self.public_ip }}3️⃣ Variables For Terraform Configurationvariables.tf helps parameterize the infrastructure, making it easier to scale or modify.variable "aws_region" { default = "us-east-1"variable "instance_type" { default = "t3.medium" default = "ami-12345678" # Replace with latest Ubuntu 22.04 AMI default = "swarm-key"4️⃣ Capturing Node IPs For AnsibleOnce Terraform provisions the EC2 instances, it outputs the IP addresses for use in the Ansible inventory file.output "manager_ips" { description = "Public IPs of Swarm manager nodes" value = aws_instance.swarm_nodes[*].public_ip} description = "Public IPs of Swarm worker nodes" value = aws_instance.swarm_nodes[*].public_ip}5️⃣ Automating Deployment With deploy.shA bash script can orchestrate Terraform and Ansible to ensure a one-command setup.#!/bin/bashecho "🚀 Deploying Terraform Infrastructure..."terraform initterraform apply -auto-approveecho "⏳ Waiting for instances to be ready..."sleep 60 # Allow time for EC2 instances to boot upecho "📌 Fetching instance IPs..."terraform output manager_ips > manager_ips.txtterraform output worker_ips > worker_ips.txtecho "📝 Generating Ansible inventory..."echo "[managers]" > ansible/inventory.inicat manager_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.iniecho "[workers]" >> ansible/inventory.inicat worker_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.iniecho "✅ Running Ansible Playbooks..."cd ansibleansible-playbook -i inventory.ini setup_swarm.ymlAutomating Swarm Cluster Configuration With AnsibleIn this section, we will use Ansible to automate the configuration of a multi-node Docker Swarm cluster.The Ansible playbooks will handle -Installing Docker on all nodes.Initializing the Swarm cluster on the manager nodes.Retrieving Swarm join tokens dynamically.Joining worker nodes to the cluster.1️⃣ Ansible Inventory File (inventory.ini)This inventory file defines the Swarm manager and worker nodes. Terraform outputs the public or private IPs, which are dynamically populated into this file.[managers]192.168.1.10192.168.1.12192.168.1.20 contains the manager nodes responsible for scheduling and orchestrating services. consists of worker nodes that run the containers but do not manage the cluster.2️⃣ Installing Docker On All Nodes (install_docker.yml)This Ansible playbook installs Docker and its dependencies on all nodes.- name: Install Docker on Swarm Nodes hosts: managers, workers tasks: - name: Update apt repositories apt: - name: Install Docker & dependencies apt: - docker.io state: present - name: Enable Docker service systemd: enabled: yes - name: Add user to Docker group user: groups: dockerUpdates the system repositories to ensure the latest packages.Installs Docker & Docker Compose on all nodes.Enables and starts the Docker service so it runs automatically.Adds the default user (ubuntu) to the Docker group to run Docker commands without sudo.3️⃣ Initializing Docker Swarm (init_swarm.yml)The first Swarm manager node must initialize the cluster.Once initialized, tokens for adding managers and workers are dynamically retrieved.- name: Initialize Docker Swarm hosts: managers tasks: - name: Initialize swarm on first manager command: "docker swarm init --advertise-addr {{ ansible_host }}" - name: Retrieve manager join token command: "docker swarm join-token manager -q" run_once: true - name: Retrieve worker join token command: "docker swarm join-token worker -q" run_once: true - name: Set manager join token as a fact set_fact: manager_join_command: "docker swarm join --token {{ manager_token.stdout }} {{ ansible_host }}:2377" - name: Set worker join token as a fact set_fact: worker_join_command: "docker swarm join --token {{ worker_token.stdout }} {{ ansible_host }}:2377"Initializes Docker Swarm on the first manager (run_once: true ensures only one manager initializes the cluster).Retrieves the join tokens for additional managers and workers.Stores the tokens as Ansible facts to be used in the next playbook.4️⃣ Adding Worker Nodes (join_swarm.yml)Once the Swarm cluster is initialized, worker nodes must join the cluster.- name: Join workers to the Swarm cluster hosts: workers tasks: - name: Join swarm as worker command: "{{ hostvars[groups['managers'][0]]['worker_join_command'] }}"Retrieves the worker join command from the first manager node.Runs the join command on all worker nodes, registering them into the cluster.5️⃣ Adding Additional Manager Nodes (join_managers.yml)If additional manager nodes are needed, they must join using the manager token.- name: Join additional managers to the Swarm cluster hosts: managers[1:] tasks: - name: Join swarm as manager command: "{{ hostvars[groups['managers'][0]]['manager_join_command'] }}"Selects all managers except the first (managers[1:]).Runs the join command to add the new managers to the Swarm cluster.6️⃣ Automating The Entire Process With deploy.shTo fully automate the Swarm setup, we create a deployment script that runs Terraform and Ansible in sequence.#!/bin/bashecho "🚀 Deploying Terraform Infrastructure..."terraform initterraform apply -auto-approveecho "⏳ Waiting for instances to be ready..."sleep 60 # Allow EC2 instances to boot upecho "📌 Fetching instance IPs..."terraform output manager_ips > manager_ips.txtterraform output worker_ips > worker_ips.txtecho "📝 Generating Ansible inventory..."echo "[managers]" > ansible/inventory.inicat manager_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.iniecho "[workers]" >> ansible/inventory.inicat worker_ips.txt | sed 's/^/ubuntu@/' >> ansible/inventory.iniecho "✅ Running Ansible Playbooks..."cd ansibleansible-playbook -i inventory.ini install_docker.ymlansible-playbook -i inventory.ini init_swarm.ymlansible-playbook -i inventory.ini join_swarm.ymlansible-playbook -i inventory.ini join_managers.ymlDeploys Terraform infrastructure.Extracts the instance IPs from Terraform outputs.Generates the Ansible inventory file dynamically.Runs all Ansible playbooks in order to set up Docker Swarm.Final Steps & Next ActionsOnce the deployment completes -✅ Docker Swarm cluster is fully configured.✅ Manager and worker nodes are added dynamically.✅ Automation reduces manual intervention and setup errors.Deploying & Managing Services In Docker SwarmOnce the Docker Swarm cluster is set up, the next step is deploying and managing containerized services efficiently.✅ Deploying a sample web application.✅ Scaling services dynamically.✅ Updating services with zero downtime.1️⃣ Deploying A Sample Web Application In SwarmTo demonstrate service deployment, we will deploy an Nginx web server across the Swarm cluster.🔹 Ansible Playbook For Deploying Nginx (deploy_nginx.yml)This Ansible playbook deploys an Nginx service with 3 replicas, ensuring high availability.- name: Deploy Web Application in Swarm hosts: managers tasks: - name: Deploy Nginx service command: > --name nginx --publish 80:80Deploys an Nginx container using docker service create.Ensures high availability with — replicas 3, meaning three instances of Nginx will run across Swarm nodes.Publishes port 80, making the service accessible via the manager node.2️⃣ Scaling Services DynamicallyAs demand increases, you may need to scale the service dynamically.This can be done using docker service scale.Scaling Nginx To 10 Replicasdocker service scale nginx=10Swarm will automatically distribute new replicas across available worker nodes.Traffic will be load-balanced among all running replicas.3️⃣ Updating Services With Zero DowntimeDocker Swarm supports rolling updates, allowing you to deploy new versions without downtime.Updating Nginx To The Latest Versiondocker service update --image nginx:latest nginxSwarm gradually replaces old containers with new ones.Ensures zero-downtime deployment by keeping some replicas online while updating others.4️⃣ Managing Service Health & StatusSwarm provides built-in commands for checking service health.docker service logs nginx5️⃣ Rolling Back A Failed DeploymentIf a new update causes issues, you can rollback to the previous version.docker service rollback nginxReverts the service to the last known stable version.Ensures minimal downtime and maintains application stability.Final Steps & Next Actions✅ Docker Swarm is now fully set up with running services.✅ Applications can be scaled dynamically based on demand.✅ Updates can be deployed with zero downtime using Swarm’s rolling updates.Monitoring & Scaling Docker SwarmEfficient monitoring and scaling are critical for maintaining a highly available and responsive Docker Swarm cluster.✅ Setting up Prometheus & Grafana for real-time monitoring.✅ Implementing auto-scaling for Swarm services based on system load.1️⃣ Setting Up Prometheus & Grafana For MonitoringPrometheus is a powerful monitoring system that collects real-time metrics, while Grafana provides a visual dashboard for better observability.🔹 Deploying Prometheus As A Swarm ServiceCreate a Prometheus configuration file (prometheus.yml) to scrape metrics from Swarm nodes.global: scrape_interval: 5s - job_name: 'docker' - targets: ['192.168.1.10:9323', '192.168.1.11:9323', '192.168.1.12:9323']🔹 Deploy Prometheus As A ServiceRun the following command to deploy Prometheus in Swarm -docker service create --name prometheus \ --publish 9090:9090 \ --mount type=bind,source=$(pwd)/prometheus.yml,target=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml \ prom/prometheus🔹 Deploying Grafana For VisualizationGrafana will provide a real-time dashboard for Swarm cluster metrics.docker service create --name grafana \ --publish 3000:3000 \ --env GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin \ grafana/grafana2️⃣ Auto-Scaling Docker Swarm ServicesDocker Swarm does not support native auto-scaling, but we can use Ansible and Prometheus alerts to dynamically adjust service replicas.🔹 Scaling Services Based On CPU UsageCreate a Prometheus alert rule (alert.rules.yml) to detect high CPU usage -groups: - name: cpu_usage_alerts - alert: HighCPUUsage expr: avg(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total[1m])) > 0.7 for: 30s severity: warning description: "High CPU usage detected on Swarm nodes."Deploy the alerting rule to Prometheus.docker service update --mount-add type=bind,source=$(pwd)/alert.rules.yml,target=/etc/prometheus/alert.rules.yml prometheus🔹 Ansible Playbook For Auto-Scaling ServicesCreate an Ansible playbook (scale_services.yml) to increase replicas dynamically.- name: Auto-Scale Swarm Services hosts: managers tasks: shell: "docker stats --no-stream --format '{{.CPUPerc}}' | awk '{s+=$1} END {print s/NR}'" - name: Scale Service if CPU > 70% command: "docker service scale nginx=10" when: cpu_load.stdout | float > 70🔹 Automating Scaling With Cron JobsTo run auto-scaling every minute, schedule the Ansible playbook using a cron job.(crontab -l ; echo "* * * * * ansible-playbook scale_services.yml") | crontab -Final Steps & Next Actions✅ Prometheus & Grafana are deployed for real-time monitoring.✅ Auto-scaling logic adjusts replicas based on CPU usage.✅ Ansible automates scaling, ensuring efficient resource usage.Common Pitfalls & Best Practices In Docker Swarm AutomationAutomating a multi-node Docker Swarm cluster using Terraform & Ansible improves scalability, but there are common pitfalls that can impact security, performance, and reliability. Below is a breakdown of the most frequent issues and best practices to mitigate them.🛠 Best Practices For Scaling & Reliability✅ — Use Terraform for provisioning, Ansible for configuration, and CI/CD pipelines for continuous deployments.✅ — Implement IAM least privilege policies, TLS encryption, and secret management with HashiCorp Vault.✅ — Monitor CPU, memory, and network usage with Prometheus, and auto-scale based on load.✅ — Set up multi-region Swarm clusters and ensure backups of container data and logs.✅ — Deploy new container versions gradually to prevent service disruptions.By following these best practices, you can scale Docker Swarm efficiently while maintaining security and reliability. 🚀Successfully deploying a highly available, multi-node Docker Swarm cluster requires a blend of infrastructure automation, configuration management, and best practices. Our DevOps teams at Xinthe leverage Terraform and Ansible to create a scalable, repeatable, and secure orchestration solution with minimal manual intervention.By combining Terraform & Ansible for Docker Swarm automation we help our enterprise clientele build resilient, scalable, and cost-efficient container orchestration solutions. Following IaC best practices, implementing automated deployments, and optimizing monitoring, will now help you too streamline multi-node Swarm deployments while maintaining security, reliability, and operational efficiency.

item.title: Serverless CI/CD: Deploying AWS Lambda Functions Automatically
item.author: Priyanshu Rajput
